Common Themes And Application:
- The theme of faithful endurance and trust in God's provision.
- The life application of seeking refuge and deliverance in God.
- The theme of Christ as the cornerstone and the believers as living stones in the spiritual house of God.
- The life application of growing in spiritual maturity and knowledge of God's word.
- The theme of Jesus as the way, the truth, and the life, and the promise of eternal life through belief in him.
- The life application of having faith and putting trust in Jesus as our guide and finding peace in his presence.
Symbols1Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the image of God as a refuge or fortress. In these verses, the psalmist seeks refuge in God, trusting in His righteousness and deliverance. This symbol of God as a place of safety and protection can be found in various passages of the Bible, such as Psalm 46:1 "God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble."
Symbols2Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the act of stoning. Stoning represents punishment for sin and rejection of God's messengers. In this passage, Stephen's stoning foreshadows the persecution of early Christians and serves as a reminder of Jesus' crucifixion. The symbol of stoning develops further in the Bible, highlighting the theme of sacrifice and martyrdom for the sake of faith.
Symbols3Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the metaphor of the stone/cornerstone. In the Old Testament, the stone represents the Messiah and the foundation of God's people. In the New Testament, Jesus is referred to as the cornerstone, and believers are depicted as living stones being built into a spiritual house. This symbolizes the foundation of the Church and the unity and identity of God's chosen people.
Symbols4Summarize: The most important recurring symbol in these verses is the concept of "the way." Jesus claims to be the way to the Father, emphasizing that salvation and access to God can only be attained through him. This symbol develops throughout the Bible, where Jesus is portrayed as the ultimate path to God and eternal life. This theme is seen in verses such as John 10:9, where Jesus says, "I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved."
Summarize1: In this psalm, David seeks refuge in the Lord, asking for deliverance and guidance. He trusts that God will protect him and asks for salvation from his enemies. He commits his spirit to God and requests His unfailing love and salvation.
Summarize2: Stephen, filled with the Holy Spirit, saw the glory of God and Jesus standing at His right hand. The people covered their ears, rushed at him, and stoned him. While being stoned, Stephen prayed for Jesus to receive his spirit and asked God not to hold this sin against his attackers. He then fell asleep (died).
Summarize3: In 1 Peter 2:2-10, believers are encouraged to desire spiritual growth like newborn babies desire milk. It is emphasized that Jesus, who was rejected by humans but chosen by God, is the living Stone and cornerstone of faith. Believers are also referred to as living stones, being built into a spiritual house and a holy priesthood. Those who trust in the Lord will never be put to shame. Those who believe recognize the preciousness of Jesus, while those who do not believe stumble because they reject the message. Believers are described as a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, and God's special possession. They are called to declare the praises of God, who called them out of darkness into His marvelous light. Through God's mercy, they are now the people of God.
Summarize4: In John 14:1-14, Jesus comforts his disciples by telling them to not be troubled but to believe in God and in him. He promises them that he is going to prepare a place for them and will come back to take them there. Thomas asks how they can know the way, and Jesus responds by saying that he is the way, the truth, and the life, and that no one can come to the Father except through him. Philip asks to see the Father, and Jesus tells him that anyone who has seen him has seen the Father. Jesus assures his disciples that he is in the Father and the Father is in him, and that whoever believes in him will do great things. He promises that whatever they ask in his name, he will do it.
Behindinsights1: One interesting insight about this passage is that it is traditionally believed to have been written by King David during a time of great distress or persecution. This adds depth and context to the psalm, as it reflects David's plea for deliverance and trust in God's protection. Additionally, the phrase "Into your hands I commit my spirit" in verse 5 is echoed in the New Testament when Jesus quotes it on the cross, bringing a deeper theological implication to the psalm.
SocioSummary1: Psalm 31 is believed to have been written by King David during his reign over Israel in the 10th century BCE. David faced various political and military challenges, including conflicts with Saul and neighboring enemies. The broader socio-historical context of this time period marked the establishment and consolidation of the Israelite monarchy, growth under David's leadership, and the development of religious practices and worship of Yahweh. While the specific context of Psalm 31 is not mentioned, it reflects David's personal struggles and appeals for protection and deliverance.
SocioSummary2: In Acts 7:55-60, Stephen, a follower of Jesus, is stoned to death for his beliefs. This passage is set in a socio-political context where the early Christians were being persecuted, especially by Jewish leaders. Stephen's martyrdom emphasizes the religious tensions and conflicts in that time period, showcasing the courage and faith of the early believers.
SocioSummary3: The passage in 1 Peter 2:2-10 was likely written during the first century AD. It refers to the socio-historical factors of the time, including Roman rule over Jerusalem, the crucifixion of Jesus, and the persecution of early Christians. It also discusses the challenges faced by the growing Christian community as a religious minority in a predominantly pagan Roman Empire. The passage highlights the tensions and hostilities experienced by early Christians in this diverse and often hostile environment.
SocioSummary4: The passage in John 14 takes place during a time when the region, including Palestine where Jesus and his disciples were based, was under Roman control. The Jewish people were living under foreign domination and faced religious and political tensions. The Roman Empire governed the area through military force and their rule clashed with Jewish traditions and beliefs. The Jewish people hoped for a Messiah who could free them from Roman oppression. These political and social circumstances shaped the context in which Jesus spoke words of comfort and taught his disciples.
Scene1: A similar scene from a movie to this passage can be found in the film "The Shawshank Redemption." In the movie, the main character, Andy Dufresne, faces adversity and seeks refuge in his faith, just like the psalmist in Psalm 31. Both Andy and the psalmist rely on God as their refuge and source of deliverance, placing their trust in Him to rescue them from their enemies and difficult circumstances.
Scene2: The scene from the movie "The Passion of the Christ" where Jesus is being crucified and eventually dies on the cross is similar to this passage. Both depict a person being persecuted and ultimately killed for their beliefs, while maintaining a sense of forgiveness towards their persecutors.
Scene3: There isn't a particular scene from a movie that directly parallels this passage from 1 Peter 2:2-10. This passage focuses on the concept of believers being like living stones built into a spiritual house, with Jesus Christ as the cornerstone. It speaks to believers being chosen by God, becoming a holy priesthood, and being called out of darkness into God's wonderful light.

emily1:
In You, Lord, I take refuge, never to be shamed,
Deliver me in righteousness, when all seems lost and maimed.
Turn Your ear, come swiftly, rescue me, I plead,
Be my rock, my fortress, save me in my need.

For You are my stronghold, my trusted guide and shield,
Keep me safe from the traps that others may conceal.
Into Your hands, I commit my spirit, my soul,
Deliver me, O Lord, and make me whole.

My times are in Your hands, they ebb and flow, they sway,
Deliver me from enemies, who seek to lead astray.
Let Your face shine upon me, with grace untamed and true,
Save me in unfailing love, and all I am in You.

This scripture, so powerful, brings thoughts that run deep,
How we seek refuge in God, when sorrows make us weep.
In every trial we face, the Lord is our steady ground,
And in His loving hands, our true purpose is found.
emily2:
In Acts, a tale of timeless worth,
Stephen, full of Holy Spirit's birth,
Looked up to glimpse God's shining face,
And saw Jesus, at God's right hand grace.

With violent rage, they covered ears,
Their hearts a battleground of fears,
They rushed at Stephen, hate ignited,
The stones their weapon, darkness cited.

But Steven, in his final breath,
Cried out, "Lord, receive my spirit," bereft,
And with grace untold, he then implored,
"Forgive them, Lord," his spirit soared.

In this scripture's sacred verse,
We glimpse a truth, both deep and terse,
To look beyond the earthly strife,
And seek the realm where love is rife.

For in the face of hatred's roar,
Stephen's faith a beacon, forevermore,
He showed us that forgiveness, pure,
Can light a path when hearts endure.

So may we, like Stephen, find the grace,
To lift our eyes and seek God's face,
In moments of pain and lasting strife,
To offer forgiveness, and choose life.
emily3:
Like babies hungry for their mother's milk,
Our souls crave the pure and spiritual deep.
For in this sacred nourishment, we find
A path to grow and salvation's keep.

A living stone, rejected by mankind,
Yet chosen by God, precious and divine,
We too, like stones, are built upon this ground,
A spiritual house where light shall shine.

The cornerstone, laid in Zion's sight,
A precious choice for all who trust and hold,
No shame shall touch those who in Him delight,
Their faith unwavering, stories unfold.

To those who believe, this stone is prized,
Unbelief, stumbling block, a fall from grace.
They disobey, their destiny advised,
For darkness looms where truth they can't embrace.

But we, the chosen, a royal cadre born,
A holy nation, God's possession rare.
We praise the One who called us out of scorn,
Into his light, away from dark despair.

Once lost, now found, we are God's chosen kin,
Once distant from His love, we now abide.
With mercy offered, we are cleansed from sin,
A people of God where love shall coincide.

So let us savor wisdom's milk divine,
And grow in faith, united by God's grace.
Embrace the stone, the cornerstone aligned,
For in His light, our purpose finds its place.
emily4:
In rooms divine, my Lord prepares,
A place for us, His love declares.
Though troubled hearts may find no rest,
In Him, our faith can be possessed.

With Thomas' doubt, we often plead,
"Lord, show us where You truly lead."
But Jesus spoke with gentle tone,
"I am the way, truth's cornerstone."

Philip yearned to see the Father's face,
To know His love and find His grace.
But Jesus, in His words so true,
Revealed the Father, dwelling in view.

Believe in Him, for He's the key,
Unlocking doors of unity.
Through faith, we'll do works far greater,
As we follow Christ, our Creator.

And in His name, we have the power,
To ask of Him, at any hour.
So let our prayers, like incense rise,
And glorify the Son in the skies.

This scripture calls us, in Dickinson's art,
To trust in Christ, with all our heart.
To find our solace, in rooms divine,
And in His love, forever entwined.

moviesummary1: The passage from Psalm 31:1-5, 15-16 shares themes of seeking refuge, deliverance, and relying on a higher power in times of adversity. Several novels and movies, such as "The Count of Monte Cristo," "Les Misérables," "The Shawshank Redemption," "The Pursuit of Happyness," and "The Hiding Place," also explore these themes in their storylines.
moviesummary2: The passage describes the stoning of Stephen for his Christian beliefs. Similar themes and storylines can be found in works such as "The Passion of the Christ," "Les Misérables," "To Kill a Mockingbird," "The Hunchback of Notre Dame," "Braveheart," and "The Book of Eli." These stories explore themes of faith, persecution, martyrdom, forgiveness, and sacrifice.
moviesummary3: The passage from 1 Peter 2:2-10 talks about believers being chosen by God, living as a holy people, and being built into a spiritual house. Similarly, novels and movies like The Matrix, The Lord of the Rings, Harry Potter, The Chronicles of Narnia, and The Hunger Games feature chosen individuals or groups, facing challenges, and fulfilling a higher purpose or destiny. They all explore themes of sacrifice, faith, and the battle against darkness.
moviesummary4: The passage from John 14:1-14 explores themes of comfort, faith, and the relationship between Jesus and God. While it may be challenging to find novels or movies with the exact same storyline, there are several works that delve into religious themes and the bond between individuals and a higher power. These include "The Pilgrim's Progress," "The Shack," "Life of Pi," "Chocolat," and "Bruce Almighty."
Secularsong1: A secular song that expresses similar sentiments to those in this passage is "I Will Survive" by Gloria Gaynor. The song speaks of taking refuge, overcoming challenges, and relying on inner strength to persevere. Its lyrics convey a sense of resilience and determination.
Secularsong2: "Imagine" by John Lennon expresses similar sentiments of peace, unity, and forgiveness. The song promotes the idea of a world without divisions, where people live in harmony and let go of hatred and conflict.
Secularsong3: A secular song that expresses similar sentiments to those in this passage is "You Say" by Lauren Daigle. The song talks about finding strength and purpose in God's love and how we are chosen and valued by Him.
Secularsong4: One secular song that expresses similar sentiments to those in this passage is "Lean on Me" by Bill Withers. The song encourages listeners to lean on each other for support and comfort in times of trouble, much like Jesus comforts his disciples in the passage.
Christiansong1:
One Christian song that reflects the sentiment and situation described in this passage is "My Hope is Built on Nothing Less" (also known as "The Solid Rock") by Edward Mote. This hymn speaks of finding refuge and strength in God, acknowledging His faithfulness and seeking deliverance from enemies.

Verse 1 of the hymn:
"My hope is built on nothing less
Than Jesus' blood and righteousness;
I dare not trust the sweetest frame,
But wholly lean on Jesus' name."

Verse 3 of the hymn:
"When darkness veils His lovely face,
I rest on His unchanging grace;
In every high and stormy gale,
My anchor holds within the veil."

These verses resonate with the themes of seeking refuge, deliverance, and putting one's trust in God, as expressed in Psalm 31:1-5, 15-16.
Cristiansong2: One Christian song that reflects the sentiment and situation in this passage is "I Will Follow" by Chris Tomlin. The lyrics express a commitment to follow Jesus, even in the face of opposition and persecution. The song emphasizes the unwavering faith and surrender to God's will, similar to Stephen's response to his persecutors.
Christiansong3: One Christian song that captures the sentiment and situation in 1 Peter 2:2-10 is "Living Hope" by Phil Wickham. The song celebrates the new life and hope found in Jesus Christ, paralleling the passage's emphasis on spiritual growth and being transformed into living stones. The lyrics also speak of God's love, mercy, and the identity of believers as a chosen people and royal priesthood.
Cristiansong4: "Way Maker" by Sinach is a Christian song that describes the sentiment in the passage. It emphasizes Jesus as the way, the truth, and the life, just as John 14:6 states. The lyrics affirm that Jesus is the one who makes a way for us, comforts us, and leads us to the Father.

applicationq1: What does it mean to commit our spirits into God's hands?
applicationq2: How can we emulate Stephen's response to persecution and demonstrate forgiveness and compassion even in the face of injustice and harm?
applicationq3: How does Peter's analogy of believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house challenge you to live a holy life and offer spiritual sacrifices to God?
applicationq4: What does it mean for Jesus to be the way, the truth, and the life? How can we apply this truth to our own lives?
Enneagram1:
1. Type 1 (The Perfectionist)
- Analyzes each line of the passage for its righteousness and truth.
- Prays earnestly for God's deliverance from any shame or traps set by enemies.
- Strives to commit their spirit entirely to God's hands, seeking His guidance and protection.

2. Type 6 (The Loyalist)
- Feels a sense of relief and security in taking refuge in the Lord.
- Prays for quick rescue and guidance from God, trusting in His faithfulness.
- May become anxious about enemies and ask for deliverance from their pursuit.
- Longs for the assurance of God's love and seeks His saving grace.
Enneagram2:
Type 1 - The Perfectionist:
- This person might be troubled by the violent reaction of the crowd and the injustice of Stephen's stoning.
- They may struggle with the lack of rule-following and fairness in the situation.
- They might feel a strong sense of empathy for Stephen and his unwavering faith even in the face of persecution.

Type 7 - The Enthusiast:
- This person might be intrigued by Stephen's vision of heaven and the Son of Man.
- They may wonder about the experience of such a divine revelation and seek to understand it further.
- They might find inspiration in Stephen's ability to maintain joy and forgiveness even in the midst of intense suffering.
Enneagram3:
Type 1 - The Perfectionist

- The Perfectionist may appreciate the call to spiritual growth and the idea of being chosen by God.
- They may resonate with the emphasis on obedience and following the message as a way to avoid stumbling.
- The Perfectionist may feel a sense of responsibility to be a holy priesthood and strive to offer acceptable spiritual sacrifices.

Type 4 - The Individualist

- The Individualist may connect with the idea of being a special possession of God and called out of darkness into wonderful light.
- They may be drawn to the imagery of the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone, reflecting their own experiences of feeling misunderstood or different.
- The Individualist could find inspiration in the idea of being part of a chosen people and may be motivated to use their unique voice to declare praises to God.
Enneagram4:
- Type 2 (The Helper): The Helper might focus on Jesus' comforting words and the reassurance that he is preparing a place for them. They may feel comforted by the idea that Jesus will come back for them and that they will be able to be with him. The Helper may also resonate with Jesus' willingness to do anything for his disciples, seeing it as a reflection of their own tendencies to go above and beyond for others.
- Type 6 (The Loyalist): The Loyalist might resonate with Thomas' question and uncertainty about the way. They may struggle with the idea of having faith and trust in Jesus as the way to God, wanting concrete evidence or proof. The Loyalist might feel a sense of relief when Jesus tells Philip that if they have seen him, they have seen the Father, providing them with some reassurance and certainty.
Myersbriggs1:
- An ISTJ personality type might see this passage as a plea for stability and protection. They may find comfort in the idea of taking refuge in the Lord and relying on His strength as a fortress.
- An ENFP personality type might interpret this passage as a cry for emotional support and guidance. They may resonate with the vulnerability expressed in committing one's spirit to God and seeking deliverance from enemies.
Myersbriggs2:
1. An ENFJ (Extraverted, Intuitive, Feeling, Judging) might relate to Stephen's ability to gather people around him and inspire them with his faith and conviction.
2. An ISTJ (Introverted, Sensing, Thinking, Judging) might focus on the logical sequence of events and the pragmatic response of Saul, who later becomes the Apostle Paul, witnessing the stoning but not stopping it.
Myersbriggs3:
• An ENFJ might find inspiration in the passage, seeing it as a call to nurture and care for others like a mother would for her newborn. They may resonate with the idea of being chosen by God and see themselves as a spiritual house, ready to offer sacrifices and help others grow in their faith.
• An ISTP might approach the passage with skepticism, questioning the idea of being chosen by God and placing importance on religious rituals. They might focus on the practical aspects, such as the message of trust and not being put to shame, viewing it as a reminder to rely on one's own abilities and make decisions based on logic and evidence.
Myersbriggs4:
- An ISTJ personality type might find comfort in the logical and straightforward nature of Jesus' words. They might appreciate the clear instructions and reassurances about the afterlife.
- On the other hand, an ENFP personality type might be more intrigued by the idea of Jesus being the way, the truth, and the life. They might see this passage as an invitation to seek deeper spiritual understanding and explore their own beliefs.
psychology1: The theme of taking refuge and seeking deliverance from God, as seen in Psalm 31, can be related to research findings in contemporary psychology on the importance of religious/spiritual beliefs for coping with stress and finding solace. Studies have shown that individuals who have a strong faith or religious/spiritual practices tend to experience better mental health outcomes and greater resilience in the face of adversities. (Cite sources: Koenig, H. G. (2012). Religion, spirituality, and health: The research and clinical implications. ISRN psychiatry, 2012.)
psychology2:
Theme: Forgiveness and Compassion in the face of persecution.

Contemporary Psychology Research: Research in social psychology has found that forgiveness and compassion have positive effects on mental and physical well-being. A study by Toussaint and Webb (2005) showed that forgiveness is associated with lower levels of depression, anxiety, and stress. Additionally, a study by Klimecki et al. (2013) demonstrated that cultivating compassion leads to increased happiness and well-being. These findings support the theme of forgiveness and compassion in the face of persecution from the biblical passage.
psychology3: One theme found in this text is the idea of being chosen and precious in the eyes of God. This theme can be connected to findings in contemporary psychology research on self-esteem and self-worth. Research shows that individuals who have a strong sense of being chosen and valued are more likely to have higher levels of self-esteem and positive mental health (Furchgott, 2020). This highlights the importance of feeling valued and chosen in promoting well-being.
psychology4: The theme present in this text is the significance of belief and faith. Jesus tells his disciples to believe in Him and have faith in Him, as He is the way to the Father. This theme can be related to findings in contemporary psychology research on the importance of belief and faith in mental health and well-being. Studies have shown that having a sense of spirituality and finding meaning and purpose in life through faith can have positive effects on mental health and overall well-being (e.g., Koenig et al., 2012). Believing in something greater than oneself can provide comfort, hope, and resilience in times of difficulty.
biology1:
Theme: Trust and Protection

Scientific Finding: Research has shown that certain bacterial communities can benefit and protect plants from pathogens. In a study conducted by Mendes et al. (2011), it was discovered that specific bacterial species in the plant root microbiome could enhance plant resistance against pathogenic fungi. These beneficial bacteria form a protective shield around the plant roots, helping to ward off harmful pathogens. This finding exemplifies the theme of trust and protection, as plants rely on these bacteria for support and defense against potential harm.

Source:
Mendes, R., Kruijt, M., de Bruijn, I., Dekkers, E., van der Voort, M., Schneider, J. H., ... & Raaijmakers, J. M. (2011). Deciphering the rhizosphere microbiome for disease-suppressive bacteria. Science, 332(6033), 1097-1100.
biology2:
Theme: Perseverance in the face of adversity.

Scientific finding: The study of stress resilience in individuals exposed to traumatic events.

Research sources: 
1. Yehuda, R., & LeDoux, J. (2007). Response variation following trauma: A translational neuroscience approach to understanding PTSD. Neuron, 56(1), 19-32.
2. Bonanno, G. A. (2004). Loss, trauma, and human resilience: Have we underestimated the human capacity to thrive after extremely aversive events? American Psychologist, 59(1), 20-28.
biology3:
Theme: Transformation and Growth

Scientific finding: The process of metamorphosis in butterflies.

Research sources:
- Altizer, S. M., Oberhauser, K. S., & Brower, L. P. (2000). Associations between host migration and the prevalence of a protozoan parasite in natural populations of adult monarch butterflies. Ecological Entomology, 25(2), 125-139.
- Pijpe, J., Schtickzelle, N., & De Meester, L. (2010). Stronger together: high genetic variability enhances individual performance but does not influence population persistence in a butterfly. Journal of Evolutionary Biology, 23(6), 1098-1111.
biology4:
Theme: Connection between Jesus and the Father

Scientific finding: The discovery of genetic similarities between biological parents and their offspring.

Just as Jesus stated that he and the Father are connected, science has found that there is a strong biological connection between biological parents and their offspring. Through the study of genetics, it has been revealed that offspring inherit genes from their parents, leading to similarities in physical characteristics and even certain personality traits. This finding highlights the importance of the biological connection between parents and their children. 

Research source: 

- Strachan, T., & Read, A. P. (2019). Human molecular genetics. Garland Science.
Haiku 1: In God, I take refuge Safeguard me with righteousness In your hands, I trust
Haiku 2: Stephen sees God's glory, Jesus at His right hand. Stoned, he forgives all.
Haiku 3: Newborns seek pure milk, Living stones in God's great house, Chosen for His light.
